meaning: The ability to move a viewing area across an image (pan) and change the magnification level (zoom)
example: The map application allows users to pan and zoom to find their location.
example2: You can pan and zoom into the high-resolution photo to see the details.
example3: The pan and zoom feature is very smooth on the new tablet.
Explanation: Pan and zoom are two of the most basic ways to interact with visual content on a screen. 'Panning' means moving the view up, down, left, or right, as if you are moving a camera across a flat surface. 'Zooming' means moving the view closer (zoom in) or further away (zoom out). These actions are essential for looking at maps, large photos, and complex blueprints. In modern apps, this is often done with 'pinch' gestures on a touchscreen or by clicking and dragging with a mouse.
commonContext: Digital maps, photo viewers, and video editing
usageTip: Use this term when describing how a user explores a large visual space on a screen.
commonMistake: Confusing 'panning' with 'scrolling'; panning usually moves in any direction, scrolling is mostly vertical.

meaning: To move across an image (pan) and make it appear larger or smaller (zoom)
example: The map app allows you to pan and zoom to find the location.
example2: We added a pan and zoom feature to the photo gallery.
example3: You can pan and zoom during the video call to see the details.
Explanation: This is a standard feature in digital imaging, maps, and video editing. 'Panning' means moving horizontally or vertically across a larger image. 'Zooming' means changing the magnification. Together, they allow a user to explore a large piece of visual information on a small screen. This is a vital interaction for touchscreens and mouse-controlled interfaces.
commonContext: Maps, photo apps, and video editing
usageTip: Use this phrase when describing how a user interacts with visual content.
commonMistake: Saying 'zoom' when you only mean moving sideways (panning).
